title: Topic 237: The Psychology of Difficulty Settings: Pride and Accessibility
content:
Topic 237: The Psychology of Difficulty Settings: Pride and Accessibility

**ABSTRACT**

This paper embarks on a perilous journey into the heart of video game difficulty settings, a digital landscape fraught with peril, pride, and the occasional existential crisis. We dissect the psychological motivations that compel one player to select “Ultra-Nightmare” mode, a setting roughly equivalent to juggling chainsaws while blindfolded, while another blissfully chooses “Story Mode,” where the greatest challenge is remembering to blink. Through a rigorous, and frankly overdue, humorous analysis of seminal psychological theories, iconic game design philosophies, and the often-unhinged online discourse, this paper reveals the humble difficulty slider as a profound diagnostic tool for the human ego. We explore how this seemingly simple choice reflects our deepest anxieties about competence, our complex relationship with effort, and our insatiable hunger for validation. The research concludes that whether a player is a battle-hardened veteran of a thousand digital wars or a peaceful tourist in a virtual world, their choice of difficulty is a deeply personal and surprisingly revealing statement about what it means to be a consumer of entertainment in the 21st century.

**INTRODUCTION**

It is a truth universally acknowledged, at least in the hallowed halls of online gaming forums, that a gamer in possession of a new video game must be in want of a formidable challenge. Or so the prevailing narrative goes. For decades, the video game industry has presented its customers with a seemingly innocuous choice, often before the opening credits have even finished rolling: Easy, Normal, or Hard. This initial selection, however, is a loaded question, a digital Rorschach test that reveals more about the player’s psyche than they might care to admit. It is a fork in the road that leads either to a serene, narrative-driven experience or to a controller-shattering, cortisol-spiking ordeal.

The debate surrounding this choice has become a cultural flashpoint, a proxy war fought with the ferocity of a thousand suns in the comment sections of YouTube videos and the subreddits of obscure indie games. On one side stand the sentinels of suffering, the “git gud” legion, a stoic army of players who believe that true gaming satisfaction can only be forged in the fiery crucible of extreme, unforgiving difficulty. They are the digital ascetics, the virtual flagellants who find enlightenment in failure and redemption in repetition. On the other side, we have the apostles of accessibility, a compassionate fellowship who argue that the joy of gaming should be a universal right, not a privilege reserved for those with lightning-fast reflexes and an abundance of free time. They are the digital humanists, the virtual egalitarians who believe that a good story should not be held hostage by a difficult boss battle.

This paper posits that the psychology of difficulty settings is a rich and largely unexplored territory, a veritable goldmine of insights into the human condition. We will venture into this treacherous terrain to explore how the seemingly opposing forces of pride and accessibility are not, in fact, mutually exclusive, but rather two sides of the same psychological coin. By dissecting the intricate web of motivations that drive players to choose their preferred level of challenge, we can begin to understand not only the games we play but also the people we are. This expedition will take us through the hallowed, and occasionally dusty, halls of psychological theory, into the chaotic and often-hilarious trenches of online gaming communities, and behind the velvet curtain of game development, where designers grapple with the delicate and often-thankless art of balancing challenge and fun. Ultimately, we will demonstrate that the seemingly trivial choice of a difficulty setting is, in fact, a fascinating and deeply revealing reflection of our most fundamental desires for competence, mastery, and, just maybe, a little bit of well-earned ego-stroking.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

Before we can psychoanalyze the entire gaming population, it is incumbent upon us to consult the ancient scrolls of academic research, a body of work that is, thankfully, less prone to flame wars than the average gaming forum. The psychology of motivation provides a robust and surprisingly applicable framework for understanding why we willingly subject ourselves to digital trials and tribulations. One of the most influential and frequently cited concepts in this realm is Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi’s theory of “flow” [1]. Flow, in essence, is the holy grail of engagement, a mental state of being so completely immersed in an activity that the outside world fades away. It is characterized by a feeling of energized focus, full involvement, and profound enjoyment in the process of the activity itself. For a video game to induce this coveted state of flow, the challenge it presents must be perfectly, almost magically, balanced with the player’s skill level. If the game is too easy, the player succumbs to the siren song of boredom and starts scrolling through their phone. If it is too hard, the player is consumed by the gnawing beast of anxiety and starts questioning their life choices. The ideal difficulty setting, therefore, is a tightrope walk over a chasm of mediocrity, a delicate dance that keeps the player in this blissful, productive state of flow.

Building upon this foundation, the concepts of competence and mastery motivation are central to our understanding of player behavior. Psychologists have long recognized that humans possess an innate, almost primal, desire to feel competent and effective in their actions. We are, at our core, a species of problem-solvers, and video games, with their clearly defined goals and immediate, unambiguous feedback, are a perfect playground for satisfying this fundamental need. The “effort justification effect” further illuminates our often-masochistic love for a good challenge [2]. This theory, which has been demonstrated in numerous studies, posits that we place a higher value on outcomes that we had to work hard to achieve. The elation of finally defeating a notoriously difficult boss after hours of trying is, therefore, far more potent and memorable than the mild, fleeting satisfaction of breezing through an easy one. It is the psychological equivalent of a home-cooked meal tasting better than a microwave dinner, even if the ingredients are identical.

The discourse surrounding difficulty is not, of course, confined to the ivory towers of academia. The gaming community itself has produced a vast, sprawling, and often-contentious body of literature on the subject. The mantra of “git gud” (a colloquial, and grammatically dubious, command to “get good”) has become a cultural touchstone, a rallying cry for those who believe that overcoming adversity is the very essence of the gaming experience. This perspective is often associated with games like the *Dark Souls* series, which are famous for their unforgiving nature and their refusal to hold the player’s hand. However, a powerful counter-narrative has emerged in recent years, a growing movement within the community and the industry that champions the cause of accessibility. Proponents of accessibility argue, with considerable justification, that difficulty should not be a barrier that excludes players with disabilities, limited time, or simply different preferences [3]. They advocate for a more nuanced and compassionate approach to difficulty, one that offers players a range of options to tailor the experience to their individual needs and desires, much like a bespoke suit, but with more dragons.

**ANALYSIS**

At the very heart of the great difficulty debate lies a powerful, primal, and often-unacknowledged force: the human ego. The choice of a difficulty setting is not merely a practical decision about how one wishes to spend their leisure time; it is a statement of identity, a declaration of one’s place in the gaming pecking order. To select “Hard,” “Nightmare,” or “I’m Too Young to Die” is to declare oneself a “serious” gamer, a digital warrior ready to face any challenge the developers can throw at them. Conversely, to choose “Easy,” “Story Mode,” or “I’m a Big Baby” can feel like an admission of inadequacy, a confession that one is not “good enough” to experience the game as the developers “intended.” This perceived judgment, whether real or imagined, can be a powerful and often-subconscious motivator, pushing players to choose a higher difficulty than they might otherwise be comfortable with, a phenomenon we might call “ego-driven difficulty selection.”

This psychological dynamic has not gone unnoticed by the canny marketers of the video game industry. They have become adept at leveraging the player’s ego to sell games, turning difficulty into a powerful marketing tool. The “hardcore” branding of games like *Dark Souls*, *Cuphead*, and *Returnal* has cultivated a dedicated and passionate fanbase, eager to prove their mettle and earn their bragging rights. The marketing for these games often emphasizes their punishing difficulty, creating an aura of exclusivity and prestige. To beat such a game is to earn a badge of honor, a digital trophy that signifies one’s membership in an elite club of skilled and persistent players. This has a direct and measurable impact on consumer behavior, as players are not just buying a game; they are buying an identity, a sense of belonging, and a story to tell their friends.

However, there is a crucial and often-overlooked distinction to be made between “fair” and “unfair” difficulty. A fair challenge is one that can be overcome through skill, practice, and learning. The *Souls* series, for all its notoriety, is a masterclass in fair difficulty. Every enemy has a learnable pattern, every attack has a tell, and every death is a lesson. An unfair challenge, on the other hand, is one that feels arbitrary, random, or cheap. Games that rely on “gotcha” moments, instant-death traps with no warning, or enemies with unpredictable and unavoidable attacks are not challenging; they are simply frustrating. The key to good difficulty design, the secret sauce that separates the masterpieces from the mediocrities, is to make the player feel that they are in control, that their successes and failures are a direct and understandable result of their own actions. It is the difference between a demanding but fair teacher and a capricious tyrant.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES**

No discussion of difficulty in modern gaming would be complete without paying homage to the high priests of punishment, FromSoftware. Their *Souls* series, along with its spiritual successors *Bloodborne* and *Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice*, have become the gold standard for “tough but fair” game design. These games are notoriously, almost comically, difficult, yet they have sold millions of copies and spawned a massive, devoted, and occasionally masochistic community. The secret to their success lies in their meticulous and uncompromising design. Every aspect of the game, from the intricate level design to the deliberate enemy placement, is crafted to challenge the player in a way that is demanding but ultimately surmountable. The community that has grown around these games is a testament to the power of shared struggle. Players come together to share strategies, offer encouragement, and celebrate each other’s hard-won victories. The *Souls* series has demonstrated, beyond a shadow of a doubt, that a high level of difficulty, when executed with care, precision, and a touch of genius, can be a powerful tool for community building and brand loyalty.

A different, but equally insightful, case study can be found in the indie gem *Celeste*. This challenging platformer is known for its fiendishly difficult levels, which require a level of precision and timing that would make a Swiss watchmaker blush. However, *Celeste* also includes a revolutionary “Assist Mode,” which allows players to customize the difficulty to their liking. They can slow down the game speed, grant themselves infinite stamina, or even become invincible. This feature was widely and deservedly praised by players and critics alike, as it allowed a much broader audience to experience the game’s beautiful story, its memorable characters, and its breathtaking art design. *Celeste* proved, with elegant simplicity, that accessibility and challenge are not mutually exclusive. A game can be both a hardcore, finger-cramping platformer and an accessible, heartwarming narrative experience, all at the same time. It is the gaming equivalent of having your cake and eating it too, and the cake is delicious.

The tension between these two philosophies was brought into sharp and often-acrimonious focus by the “journalist mode” controversy. The term was coined, often derisively, to describe a hypothetical easy mode that would allow game journalists to quickly and efficiently play through a game for review purposes. The backlash against this idea was swift, fierce, and occasionally unhinged, with many gamers arguing that it would devalue the experience and that journalists had a sacred responsibility to play the game as it was “meant to be played.” This controversy revealed a deep-seated anxiety within certain segments of the gaming community about the perceived legitimacy of their hobby. For many, the difficulty of games is a source of pride, a way of distinguishing their pastime from more “casual” forms of entertainment like film or television. It is a form of cultural gatekeeping, a way of saying, “You have to be this tall to ride this ride.”

**DISCUSSION**

What, then, are the implications of this complex psychological web for the average consumer, the everyday player who just wants to unwind after a long day at work? Are we, as players, doomed to a perpetual cycle of frustration, fleeting triumph, and the occasional thrown controller? Not necessarily. The growing and increasingly mainstream conversation around accessibility is a profoundly positive sign that the industry is beginning to recognize the diversity of its audience. The future of difficulty may lie not in a simple, rigid, three-tiered system but in a more granular, flexible, and customizable approach. Dynamic difficulty adjustment, a technology that automatically and invisibly tailors the challenge to the player’s performance, is one promising and exciting avenue. Another is the proliferation of “a la carte” difficulty options, similar to *Celeste’s* groundbreaking Assist Mode, which empower players to pick and choose the specific aspects of the game they want to make easier or harder. Imagine being able to turn down the enemy’s health while keeping their AI aggressive, or to increase the window for parrying without affecting the damage you take. The possibilities are as endless as they are exciting.

Furthermore, the psychology of difficulty has implications that extend far beyond the glowing rectangle of our television screens. Our relationship with challenge in the virtual world can be a surprisingly accurate mirror for how we approach challenges in our real, non-virtual lives. Do we seek out difficult tasks in our careers and hobbies, or do we prefer a more comfortable, predictable, and less stressful path? Do we view failure as a valuable learning experience, a stepping stone to future success, or as a personal failing, a reflection of our own inadequacy? The way we answer these questions, both in the games we play and in the lives we lead, can reveal a great deal about our personalities, our values, and our overall approach to the grand, unscripted game of life.

**CONCLUSION**

In the grand, sprawling, and often-chaotic tapestry of human experience, the choice between “Easy” and “Hard” on a video game menu may seem like a trivial, insignificant thread. Yet, as we have seen, it is a thread that is interwoven with some of the most fundamental and fascinating aspects of our psychology: our deep-seated desire for competence, our insatiable need for validation, and our complex, often-contradictory relationship with the very concept of effort. The debate between pride and accessibility is not, and should not be, a zero-sum game. A well-designed, thoughtfully crafted game can and should be able to cater to both the hardcore, battle-scarred player seeking a formidable challenge and the casual, time-poor player looking for a relaxing and engaging escape. As the video game industry continues to evolve and mature, it is our fervent hope that it will embrace a more inclusive, empathetic, and nuanced approach to difficulty, one that recognizes and celebrates the vast and varied landscape of the human psyche. After all, the ultimate goal of any game, from the simplest mobile puzzler to the most complex open-world epic, should be to bring joy, not to serve as a digital gatekeeper. And if that joy is found in the triumphant conquering of a seemingly impossible boss or in the simple, unadulterated pleasure of enjoying a beautiful story, who are we to judge?
references:
[1] Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). *Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience*. Harper & Row.

[2] Festinger, L. (1957). *A Theory of Cognitive Dissonance*. Stanford University Press.

[3] Can I Play That? (2021, December 17). *Difficulty in Video Games is Accessibility*. https://caniplaythat.com/2021/12/17/difficulty-in-video-games-is-accessibility/
